content: {"perspective": "Industry", "answer": "The search results confirm and expand on the JMIR 52-week observational study of IF mobile apps. Source 9 (ResearchGate preprint 357413255) discloses the most important detail: 'Intermittent Fasting with the LIFE mobile apps appears to be a sustainable approach to weight reduction' — establishing that the studied apps are the LIFE family, not Zero. Sources 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 are the published JMIR mHealth uHealth 2022 paper (Torres et al. / JMIR 10(10):e35896, PMID 36194463). The most concrete weight-loss finding comes from Sources 1 and 4: 'Users with a BMI ≥40 kg/m2 lost 13.9% of their starting weight by 52 weeks versus a slight weight gain on average for users with starting BMI < [...]' — a striking BMI-stratified effect, with high-BMI users achieving large weight loss and lower-BMI users showing no benefit or slight gain. This stratification is critical: it shows the real-world IF app population benefits mostly users who are severely obese, and actively contradicts the idea that IF produces uniform weight loss across all consumers. Source 3 and Source 11 (ResearchGate preprint 362290711) confirm that 'Retention for up to 52 weeks was quantified based on recorded fasts and correlated with user demographics. Users who provided height and at least two weights and...' — meaning retention was operationalized as recorded-fasts activity rather than subscription renewal. Source 10 (PMC11091450 SIMPLE app paper) cross-cites the same JMIR study. Information gap: the snippets do NOT disclose the exact sample size (the N for the retention cohort), do NOT show a week-by-week retention curve, do NOT break down protocol usage (16:8 vs 5:2 vs ADF), do NOT correlate fasting hours with weight loss quantitatively, and do NOT report any cardiovascular or adverse-event data. The BMI stratification — 13.9% weight loss in BMI ≥40 vs slight gain in lower BMI — is the most actionable real-world finding for the synthesis and is a notable counter to generic IF marketing that implies everyone benefits.", "key_findings": ["The JMIR 52-week study focused on the LIFE family of IF mobile apps (Source 9, ResearchGate 357413255), not Zero — correcting the round-1 assumption.", "BMI-stratified weight loss (Sources 1, 4, mhealth.jmir.org/2022/10/e35896, PMID 36194463): users with BMI ≥40 kg/m² lost 13.9% of starting weight by 52 weeks, while users with lower starting BMI had slight weight GAIN on average — real-world benefit is highly BMI-dependent.", "Retention operationalized as recorded-fasts activity (Sources 3, 11), not subscription renewal — an important methodological detail: these are engaged users who keep using the app, not a random population sample.", "The concrete weight-loss stratification counters generic IF marketing: the app population benefits mostly users with severe obesity, and users with lower BMI may gain weight on average — a substantial real-world nuance.", "Information gap: sample size N, week-by-week retention curve, protocol breakdown (16:8 vs 5:2 vs ADF), and any safety/adverse-event data are NOT in the recovered snippets."], "sources_used": ["https://mhealth.jmir.org/2022/10/e35896", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9579929/", "https://www.researchgate.net/publication/362290711_Retention_Fasting_Patterns_and_Weight_Loss_With_an_Intermittent_Fasting_App_A_Large-scale_52-week_Observational_Study_Preprint", "https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36194463/", "https://scholarworks.indianapolis.iu.edu/items/6095d29a-36d4-4a22-ba10-620143ea1696", "https://www.researchgate.net/publication/357413255_Retention_Fasting_Patterns_and_Weight_Loss_With_an_Intermittent_Fasting_App_A_Large-scale_52-week_Observational_Study_Preprint", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11091450/"]}
